title: Functions Working Together

learningObjectives.text: Explain functions calling functions using a new example and justify the result.
Explain function inputs and outputs using a new example and justify the result.
Apply function review and composition using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in functions working together recur throughout later JavaScript work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **Orientation and Foundations** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Functions calling functions** → **Function inputs and outputs** → **Function review and composition**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Functions calling functions
sections.body: Functions can delegate to other functions, turning a large operation into named, testable transformations. The caller coordinates the sequence while each helper owns one responsibility.

sections.title: Function inputs and outputs
sections.body: Parameters define what a function needs; return values define what it produces. Explicit inputs and outputs reduce hidden dependencies and make functions easier to compose and test.

sections.title: Function review and composition
sections.body: Composition builds larger behavior from smaller functions whose outputs can become later inputs. Even without a composition utility, the principle encourages narrow responsibilities and predictable data flow.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: One function delegates validation to another and uses the returned value.

commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.
summary: Functions Working Together connects Functions calling functions, Function inputs and outputs, Function review and composition.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

choices.label: HTML describes structure, CSS describes presentation, and JavaScript expresses behavior and application logic. It responds to events, changes the document, communicates with servers, and coordinates state.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions calling functions.

choices.label: A Promise begins pending and settles once as fulfilled or rejected. then, catch, and finally schedule reactions asynchronously.

choices.label: DOM APIs locate nodes by selector or relationship, then update text, attributes, classes, styles, or structure. Prefer focused updates and class changes over scattered inline mutations.

questions.prompt: A learner must use Functions Working Together in a new situation. Which approach best demonstrates transferable understanding?

choices.label: Skip the underlying model and rely on memorized syntax, even when the result is unclear.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

choices.label: Treat the concept as isolated trivia and avoid connecting it to earlier prerequisites.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

choices.label: Apply Functions calling functions deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.
choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.

choices.label: Add more state and abstraction immediately, before identifying the smallest requirement.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

questions.explanation: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
questions.hint: Choose the option that would still work when the surrounding example changes.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

choices.label: The statement is always correct, so no further reasoning about Functions Working Together is needed.

choices.label: The statement is correct only when variable names are short.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

choices.label: The statement is a misconception. A better approach is to reason from the concept's inputs, rules, and observable outcome, then verify the result.
choices.feedback: Good feedback names the misconception and replaces it with a usable reasoning process.

choices.label: The statement is unrelated to program behavior and cannot cause defects.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Functions Working Together?

choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Functions calling functions, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.
choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.

choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.

questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Functions Working Together?

choices.label: Avoid Functions Working Together completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

choices.label: Use Functions Working Together when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.
choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

choices.label: Use Functions Working Together in every file because more abstraction is always better.
choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Functions Working Together.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
